name: silybum marianum
mechanism: Proposed mechanism: silymarin mixture is the named major constituent (NCCIH); mechanistic pathways are not established as strong clinical mechanisms in EMA traditional-use monograph, and human efficacy remains conflicting/limited.
activeCompounds: Silymarin (mixture of compounds) as main constituent of extract.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless clinician-guided
Use caution with prescription medications and chronic conditions; monitor for intolerance.
safetyNotes: NCCIH: generally well tolerated orally; common side effects are digestive symptoms; allergic reactions possible especially in those allergic to related plants; little is known about safety in pregnancy/breastfeeding. NCCIH raises concerns about poor chemical/microbiological quality and contamination/label mismatch in commercial supplements. EMA monograph lists GI symptoms and allergic reactions as possible undesirable effects and notes Asteraceae allergy contraindication; pregnancy/lactation not recommended due to insufficient data.

sources.title: EU herbal monograph on *Silybum marianum* (L.) Gaertn., fructus (EMA)

description: NCCIH identifies silymarin (mixture of compounds) as the main constituent of milk thistle extract; EMA monograph specifies multiple preparation types (tea, powdered herb, various dry/soft extracts with DER and solvents).
dosage: EMA posology (adults/elderly) includes: tea 3–5 g in 100 mL boiling water 2–3×/day; powdered herb 300–600 mg 2–3×/day (daily up to 1800 mg); multiple extract-specific single-dose ranges by DER/solvent.

interactions: EMA: none reported. NCCIH advises consulting clinicians because herb–medicine interactions can occur but does not provide a definitive interaction list for milk thistle in the fact sheet.
preparation: Tea (comminuted fruit), powdered substance, dry extracts (multiple solvents/DER ranges), soft extract; oral use.
region: Native range (POWO): Macaronesia, Mediterranean to Central Asia and India, Ethiopia.
therapeuticUses: historical use for symptom relief in regional folk medicine
sideEffects: mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or loose stool)
allergic rash or itching in sensitive individuals
summary: Marketing claims commonly target liver disorders and metabolic outcomes; NCCIH states there is insufficient high-quality evidence for definitive conclusions in people, with mixed/limited clinical trial results for liver diseases and some evidence suggesting possible blood sugar effects in type 2 diabetes (with generalizability limitations). EMA monograph frames use as traditional for digestive disorders and "support liver function" after serious conditions are excluded by a medical doctor.
